question_text: In the form of dichromate, Cr (VI) is a strong oxidising agent in acidic medium but Mo (VI) in MoO 3 and W(VI) in WO 3 are not because ________. Cr (VI) is more stable than Mo(VI) and W(VI). Mo(VI) and W(VI) are more stable than Cr(VI). Higher oxidation states of heavier members of group-6 of transition series are more stable. Lower oxidation states of heavier members of group-6 of transition series are more stable.
question_html:
In the form of dichromate, Cr (VI) is a strong oxidising agent in acidic medium but Mo (VI) in MoO3 and W(VI) in WO3 are not because ________.

  1. Cr (VI) is more stable than Mo(VI) and W(VI).
  2. Mo(VI) and W(VI) are more stable than Cr(VI).
  3. Higher oxidation states of heavier members of group-6 of transition series are more stable.
  4. Lower oxidation states of heavier members of group-6 of transition series are more stable.
solution_text: In d-block elements, for heavier elements, the higher oxidation states are more stable. Hence, (Molybdenum) Mo(VI) and (Wolfram) W(Vl) are more stable than Cr(Vl). That's why, Cr(Vl) in the form of dichromate is a stronger oxidising agent in acidic medium whereas MoO 3 and WO 3 are not, therefore (ii) and (iii) are correct options.
solution_html: In d-block elements, for heavier elements, the higher oxidation states are more stable. Hence, (Molybdenum) Mo(VI) and (Wolfram) W(Vl) are more stable than Cr(Vl). That's why, Cr(Vl) in the form of dichromate is a stronger oxidising agent in acidic medium whereas MoO3 and WO3 are not, therefore (ii) and (iii) are correct options.

question_text: Why does copper not replace hydrogen from acids?
question_html: Why does copper not replace hydrogen from acids?
solution_text: The unique behaviour of Cu, having a positive E o (Cu +2 /Cu) = 0.34V, accounts for its inability to liberate H 2 from acids. Only oxidising acids (nitric and hot concentrated sulphuric) react with Cu, the acids being reduced. The high energy to transform Cu(s) to Cu 2+ (aq) is not balanced by its hydration enthalpy of Cu +2 .
solution_html: The unique behaviour of Cu, having a positive Eo (Cu+2/Cu) = 0.34V, accounts for its inability to liberate H2 from acids. Only oxidising acids (nitric and hot concentrated sulphuric) react with Cu, the acids being reduced. The high energy to transform Cu(s) to Cu2+(aq) is not balanced by its hydration enthalpy of Cu+2.

question_text: Why {tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex} values for Mn, Ni, and Zn are more negative than expected?
question_html: Why {tex}E^{\ominus}{/tex} values for Mn, Ni, and Zn are more negative than expected?
solution_text: Negative E o values of Mn 2+ and Zn 2+ are because of stabilities of half-filled d subshell (3d 5 : Mn 2+ ) and fully-filled d subshell in (3d 10 : Zn 2+ ) configuration respectively. Ni 2+ ion has a higher E o value because of the highest negative enthalpy of hydrogen.

question_text: Why first ionisation enthalpy of chromium is lower than that of Zn?
question_html: Why first ionisation enthalpy of chromium is lower than that of Zn?
solution_text: The electronic configuration of chromium and zinc are respectively: Cr(24) = [Ar] 3d 5 4s 2 Zn(30) = [Ar]3d 10 4s 2 It is easy to remove electron from 4 s 1 -orbital (unpaired) rather than from 4s 2 -orbital (paired). Therefore, first ionisation enthalpy of Cr is less than Zn.
solution_html:
The electronic configuration of chromium and zinc are respectively:
Cr(24) = [Ar] 3d54s2
Zn(30) = [Ar]3d104s2
It is easy to remove electron from 4 s1 -orbital (unpaired) rather than from 4s2 -orbital (paired). Therefore, first ionisation enthalpy of Cr is less than Zn.

question_text: Although fluorine is more electronegative than oxygen, but the ability of oxygen to stabilise higher oxidation states exceeds that of fluorine. Why?
question_html: Although fluorine is more electronegative than oxygen, but the ability of oxygen to stabilise higher oxidation states exceeds that of fluorine. Why?
solution_text: The electronic configuration of fluorine (F) is 1s 2 2s 2 2p 5 . Thus it can form only one bond as it has only one unpaired electron. Electronic configuration of oxygen (O) is 1s 2 2s 2 2p 6 3s 2 3p 6 . It may be mentioned that oxygen also has vacant d-orbitals along with two 3 p orbital containing a single electron. Thus, oxygen has a greater bond formation capacity to metal. In other words, it has a greater ability to stabilize higher oxidation states that exceeds that of fluorine.
solution_html:
The electronic configuration of fluorine (F) is 1s2 2s2 2p5. Thus it can form only one bond as it has only one unpaired electron. Electronic configuration of oxygen (O) is 1s22s2 2p63s23p6.
It may be mentioned that oxygen also has vacant d-orbitals along with two 3 p orbital containing a single electron. Thus, oxygen has a greater bond formation capacity to metal. In other words, it has a greater ability to stabilize higher oxidation states that exceeds that of fluorine.

However, appreciable contribution occurs in Co 2+ ion. Therefore, magnetic moment variation occur.

question_text: Ionisation enthalpies of Ce, Pr, and Nd are higher than Th, Pa, and U. Why?
question_html: Ionisation enthalpies of Ce, Pr, and Nd are higher than Th, Pa, and U. Why?
solution_text: Ce, Pr, and Nd lie in the 6th group. While, Th, Pa, and U lie in the 7th group. In the beginning, when 5f -orbitals begin to be occupied, they penetrate less into inner core electrons. Thus, 5f-electrons are more effectively shielded from the nuclear charge than 4f electrons in the lanthanide series. Therefore, outer electrons are less firmly held and thus these electrons are available for bonding in actinoids and their removal is much easier due to their bigger size. On account of this, the ionization enthalpies of Th, Pa, and U are comparatively lower than Ce, Pr, and Nd.

question_text: Although Zr belongs to 4d and Hf belongs to 5d transition series but it is quite difficult to separate them. Why?
question_html: Although Zr belongs to 4d and Hf belongs to 5d transition series but it is quite difficult to separate them. Why?
solution_text: Zr, a member of the 4d series and Hf, a member of the 5d series, belong to the same group. Their chemical and physical properties are so similar that their separation is difficult. This is due to the fact that both have the same atomic size due to lanthanide contraction. The atomic radii of these elements are 160 pm(Zr) and 159 pm(Hf).

This is due to the fact that both have the same atomic size due to lanthanide contraction. The atomic radii of these elements are 160 pm(Zr) and 159 pm(Hf).

question_html: A solution of KMnO4 on reduction yields either a colorless solution or a brown precipitate or a green solution depending on pH of the solution. What different stages of the reduction do these represent and how are they carried out?
solution_text: Potassium permanganate (KMnO 4 ) acts as an oxidising agent in alkaline, neutral and acidic mediums, i.e., oxidising behaviour of (potassium permanganate) KMnO 4 depends on pH of the solution. In an alkaline medium (pH > 7).-+ {tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} i.e., permanganate is changed to manganate K 2 MnO 4 which gives a green solution. {tex}2{\text{KMn}}{{\text{O}}_4} + 2{\text{KOH}} \to \mathop {2{{\text{K}}_2}{\text{Mn}}{{\text{O}}_4}}\limits_{Green} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} + [{\text{O}}]{/tex} Reducing agent +[O] {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Product In a neutral solution (pH = 7), permanganate is changed to manganese dioxide MnO 2 (brown ppt.) {tex}{\text{MnO}}_4^ - + 2{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} \to \mathop {{\text{Mn}}{{\text{O}}_2}}\limits_{Brown\,ppt.} + 4{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }{/tex} 2KMnO 4 + H 2 O {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2KOH + 2MnO 2 + 3[O] In acidic medium (pH<7), permanganate is changed to manganous ion Mn (Colourless). {tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+8 \mathrm{H}^{-} \mathrm{ +5e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex} {tex}2{\text{KMn}}{{\text{O}}_4} + 3{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4} \to \mathop {2{\text{MnS}}{{\text{O}}_4}}\limits_{Colourless\,solution} + {{\text{K}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4} + 3{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} + 5[{\text{O}}]{/tex}

{tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex}
i.e., permanganate is changed to manganate K2MnO4 which gives a green solution.

Reducing agent +[O] {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Product
In a neutral solution (pH = 7), permanganate is changed to manganese dioxide MnO2 (brown ppt.)
{tex}{\text{MnO}}_4^ - + 2{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} \to \mathop {{\text{Mn}}{{\text{O}}_2}}\limits_{Brown\,ppt.} + 4{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }{/tex}

question_text: The halides of transition elements become more covalent with increasing oxidation state of the metal. Why?
question_html: The halides of transition elements become more covalent with increasing oxidation state of the metal. Why?
solution_text: As the oxidation state of the element increases, the size of transition metal cations decreases its charge increases. According to Fajans rules, as the charge of the metal ion increases covalent character increases because the positively charged cation attracts the electron cloud on the anion towards itself.

question_text: While filling up of electrons in the atomic orbitals, the 4s orbital is filled before the 3d orbital but the reverse happens during the ionization of the atom. Explain why?
question_html: While filling up of electrons in the atomic orbitals, the 4s orbital is filled before the 3d orbital but the reverse happens during the ionization of the atom. Explain why?
solution_text: According to n + 1 rule: For 3d = n + 1 = 5 4s = n + 1 = 4 Therefore, the electron will enter in 4s orbital first and then in 3d orbitals. ionization enthalpy is responsible for the ionization of atom. The electron present in 4s orbital are loosely held by the nucleus. So electrons are removed from 4s orbital prior to 3d orbital.

question_text: On the basis of Lanthanoid contraction, explain the following : Nature of bonding in La 2 O 3 and Lu 2 O 3 . Trends in the stability of oxo salts of lanthanoids from La to Lu. Stability of the complexes of lanthanoids. Radii of 4d and 5d block elements. Trends in the acidic character of lanthanoid oxides.
question_html:
On the basis of Lanthanoid contraction, explain the following :

  1. Nature of bonding in La2O3 and Lu2O3 .
  2. Trends in the stability of oxo salts of lanthanoids from La to Lu.
  3. Stability of the complexes of lanthanoids.
  4. Radii of 4d and 5d block elements.
  5. Trends in the acidic character of lanthanoid oxides.
solution_text: On the basis of lanthanoid contraction: Due to lanthanide contraction, the size of the atom decreases with the decrease in size, the covalent character increases. Therefore, La 2 O 3 is more ionic while Lu 2 O 3 is more covalent in nature. As the size decreases from (lanthanum) La to (lutetium) Lu, the stability of the oxo- salts also decreases. Stability of complexes from La to Lu, increases as the size of the central atom decreases. Radii of the elements of 4d-and 5d-blocks in the same vertical columns are nearly the same. The acidic nature of lanthanoid oxides increases from La to Lu.
solution_html:
On the basis of lanthanoid contraction:

  1. Due to lanthanide contraction, the size of the atom decreases with the decrease in size, the covalent character increases. Therefore, La2O3 is more ionic while Lu2O3 is more covalent in nature.
  2. As the size decreases from (lanthanum) La to (lutetium) Lu, the stability of the oxo- salts also decreases.
  3. Stability of complexes from La to Lu, increases as the size of the central atom decreases.
  4. Radii of the elements of 4d-and 5d-blocks in the same vertical columns are nearly the same.
  5. The acidic nature of lanthanoid oxides increases from La to Lu.

question_text: Mention the type of compounds formed when small atoms like H, C, and N get trapped inside the crystal lattice of transition metals. It also gives the physical and chemical characteristics of these compounds.
question_html: Mention the type of compounds formed when small atoms like H, C, and N get trapped inside the crystal lattice of transition metals. It also gives the physical and chemical characteristics of these compounds.
solution_text: Compounds formed when small atoms like H, C, and N get trapped inside the crystal lattice of transition metals are called interstitial compounds. The physical and chemical properties of such compounds are: They have High melting points higher than those of pure metals. These compounds are very hard e.g., diamond. They retain metallic conductivity. They are chemically inert.
solution_html:
Compounds formed when small atoms like H, C, and N get trapped inside the crystal lattice of transition metals are called interstitial compounds. The physical and chemical properties of such compounds are:

  1. They have High melting points higher than those of pure metals.
  2. These compounds are very hard e.g., diamond.
  3. They retain metallic conductivity.
  4. They are chemically inert.

solution_text: Transition metals can act as catalysts because these can change their oxidation state. The reaction between iodide and persulphate ions catalyzes by Fe is as follows: {tex}2{{\text{I}}^ - } + {{\text{S}}_2}{\text{O}}_8^{2 - }\xrightarrow{{{\text{Fe}}({\text{III}})}}{{\text{I}}_2} + 2{\text{SO}}_4^{2 - }{/tex} Role of Fe(III) ions: 2Fe 3+ + 2I - {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Fe 2+ + I 2 {tex}2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}^{2-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex}
solution_html:
Transition metals can act as catalysts because these can change their oxidation state. The reaction between iodide and persulphate ions catalyzes by Fe is as follows:
{tex}2{{\text{I}}^ - } + {{\text{S}}_2}{\text{O}}_8^{2 - }\xrightarrow{{{\text{Fe}}({\text{III}})}}{{\text{I}}_2} + 2{\text{SO}}_4^{2 - }{/tex}
Role of Fe(III) ions:
2Fe3+ + 2I- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2Fe2+ + I2
{tex}2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8}^{2-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+2 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex}

question_html: A violet compound of manganese (A) decomposes on heating to liberate oxygen and compounds (B) and (C) of manganese are formed. Compound (C) reacts with KOH in the presence of oxygen to give compound (B). On heating compound (C) with concentrated {tex}H_2SO_4{/tex} and NaCl, chlorine gas is liberated and a compound (D) of manganese along with other products is formed. Identify A to D and also explain the reactions involved.
solution_text: {tex}\mathop {KMn{O_4}}\limits_{(A)} \xrightarrow{{Heat}}\mathop {{K_2}Mn{O_4}}\limits_{(B)} + \mathop {Mn{O_2}}\limits_{(C)} + {O_2}{/tex} {tex}Mn{O_2} + KOH + {O_2} \to 2{K_2}Mn{O_4} + 2{H_2}O{/tex} {tex}Mn{O_2} + 4NaCl + 4{H_2}S{O_4} \to{/tex} {tex}\mathop {MnC{l_2}}\limits_{(D)} + 2NaHS{O_4} + C{l_2} + {H_2}O{/tex} From the above set of reaction we can deduce that: (A) = KMnO 4 (B) = K 2 MnO 4 (C) = MnO 2 (D) = MnCl 2
solution_html:
{tex}\mathop {KMn{O_4}}\limits_{(A)} \xrightarrow{{Heat}}\mathop {{K_2}Mn{O_4}}\limits_{(B)} + \mathop {Mn{O_2}}\limits_{(C)} + {O_2}{/tex}
{tex}Mn{O_2} + KOH + {O_2} \to 2{K_2}Mn{O_4} + 2{H_2}O{/tex}
{tex}Mn{O_2} + 4NaCl + 4{H_2}S{O_4} \to{/tex}{tex}\mathop {MnC{l_2}}\limits_{(D)} + 2NaHS{O_4} + C{l_2} + {H_2}O{/tex}

From the above set of reaction we can deduce that:
(A) = KMnO4
(B) = K2MnO4
(C) = MnO2
(D) = MnCl2
